Run CoPro on brain MERFISH data

2024-08-07

Content

This Vignette contains an example to run CoPro on a brain MERFISH data. The data is from Zhang et al. Nature 2023 (see reference 1). Here, we focus on detecting the co-progression between D1 and D2 neurons, as reported in the original publication. Here, we aim to show the unsupervised detection of continuous and coordinated cell progressions and how that is related to the tissue structure.

load packages

library("CoPro")
library_paths <- c('fields', 'ggplot2', 'tictoc', 'irlba', 
                   'Matrix', 'ggsci', 'ape')
# Load required libraries
lapply(library_paths, library, character.only = TRUE)

set some parameters

We can change the number of PCs. For this dataset, this parameter does not affect the results much

n_pca = 40 ## number of top PCs to retain
cell_types = c('061 STR D1 Gaba', '062 STR D2 Gaba')
n_cell_types = length(cell_types)

horizontal_dist_scale <- 1
vertical_dist_scale <- 1

Load data

Here we load the normalized gene matrix as well as the meta data. The cell type label as well as cell location information are in the meta data.

ra <- readRDS('/Users/zhenmiao/Dropbox/Zhuang-ABCA-1.054_1/Zhuang_ABCA_1.054_subset_data.rds')
meta <- readRDS('/Users/zhenmiao/Dropbox/Zhuang-ABCA-1.054_1/Zhuang_ABCA_1.054_subset_metadata.rds')

subset data

subset_cells <- meta$subclass %in% cell_types 
  
ra = ra[subset_cells,]
meta = meta[subset_cells,]
rownames(meta) <- meta$cell_label
## make sure coordinates are numeric 
meta$x = as.numeric(meta$x)
meta$y = as.numeric(meta$y)

show the in situ plot

ggplot(data = meta)+
  geom_point(aes(x = x, y = y, color = subclass))+
  theme_minimal()+
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1)

load data into object

location_data <- meta[,c('x','y')]
cell_type_data <- meta[,'subclass']
br <- newCoPro(normalizedData = ra,
               locationData = location_data,
               metaData = meta, 
               cellTypes = cell_type_data
                      )
br <- subsetData(br, cellTypesOfInterest = cell_types)

compute distances

br <- computePCA(br, nPCA = n_pca, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
sigma_square_choice = c(0.001, 0.002, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05,0.1, 0.2,0.5)
br <- computeDistance(br, distType = "Euclidean2D")
br <- computeKernelMatrix(br, sigmaSquares = sigma_square_choice)
## Current Sigma value is  0.001Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##    0   18   23   27   57 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##    2   22   27   32   62 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.002Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##    0   33   40   47   87 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##   10   40   49   56   90 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.005Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##    2   71   86   97  150 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##   30   84  103  115  158 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.01Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##   22  115  152  167  232 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##   62  140  170  198  240 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.02Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##    0%   25%   50%   75%  100% 
##  57.0 185.0 251.5 285.0 348.0 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##   84  224  274  334  390 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.05Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##   97  333  456  553  673 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##  154  399  520  629  751 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.1Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##      0%     25%     50%     75%    100% 
##  191.00  503.75  681.00  839.00 1045.00 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##  302  597  796  962 1194 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.2Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##      0%     25%     50%     75%    100% 
##  354.00  734.75  970.00 1209.00 1567.00 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##  517  879 1134 1403 1800 
## 
## Current Sigma value is  0.5Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 061 STR D1 Gaba 
##      0%     25%     50%     75%    100% 
##  704.00 1119.00 1393.50 1668.25 2151.00 
## Quantiles of N_neighbors for cell type 062 STR D2 Gaba 
##   0%  25%  50%  75% 100% 
##  965 1334 1639 1935 2376

run skrCCA and obtain correlations

br <- runSkrCCA(br,scalePCs = TRUE,maxIter = 500)
## [1] "convergence reached at 42 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 44 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 37 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 22 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 21 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 15 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 12 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 12 iterations"
## [1] "convergence reached at 9 iterations"
br <- computeNormalizedCorrelation(br)

compute gene and cell scores

br <- computeGeneAndCellScores(br)

Look across different sigma values

Here, we hope to pick the sigma value with the highest normalized correlation value. We visualize the trend using ggplot2

ncorr <- getNormCorr(br)

ggplot(data = ncorr, aes(x = sigmaSquares, y = normalizedCorrelation, group = 1)) +
      geom_point()+
      geom_line() +
      facet_wrap(vars(ct12)) +
      xlab("Sigma squared") +
      ylab("Norm. Corr.") +
      ggtitle(label = "Norm. Corr. across sigma squared values") +
      theme_minimal()

From the figure above, the highest correlation is when sigma^2 = 0.02, so we use this sigma value for the downstream analyses

Cell scores in situ

We can retrive the data.frame that contains the cell scores using the following function:

br_cs <- getCellScoresInSitu(br, sigmaSquaredChoice = 0.02,
                             scoreColorType = "binary")

We can visualize the cell scores in situ using ggplot.

ggplot(data = br_cs)+
      geom_point(aes(x = x,
                     y = y,
                     color = cellScores_b), size = 0.8)+
      scale_color_discrete(type = c('darkgreen','#d4f8d4','darkred','#ffd8d8'))+
      coord_fixed()+
      theme_minimal()

We can also visualize the continuous scores

ggplot(data = br_cs)+
      geom_point(aes(x = x,
                     y = y,
                     color = cellScores), size = 0.8)+
      coord_fixed()+
      theme_minimal()

References:

Zhang, M., Pan, X., Jung, W. et al. Molecularly defined and spatially resolved cell atlas of the whole mouse brain. Nature 624, 343–354 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-023-06808-9
